feedback.text: The Act currently does not cover the scenario of when publishers release video game products that rely on a connection to an Internet server for scenarios such as game content or online multiplayer. When said Internet servers are shut down at the end of a game's lifecycle, this results in a consumer's inability to play the product they paid for, as the servers the game depends on no longer exist. Solutions for such a problem can include requiring video game developers to offer offline modes for video-games or requiring video game developers to provide server software that can be used by the video game community to create replacement servers in order to unlock the online content again. This is a significant issue as many video games are entirely unplayable as a result of this problem. The most notable case in recent times has been The Crew, which was rendered unplayable on the 31st of March 2024, when Ubisoft shut down the game's servers with no plans for remediation. I therefore urge the European Commission to consider adding legislation to cover this case in order to protect consumers' statutory rights to use the product they paid for.
